1: 'I will bring one more plague on Pharaoh and on Egypt. After that, he will let you go from here, and when he does, he will drive you out completely.' The announcement of the final, decisive plague. The 'one more' is the culminating act — everything before has been leading here. The cross is the NT's 'one more': 'once for all he entered the holy places, not by means of the blood of goats and calves but by means of his own blood, thus securing an eternal redemption' (Hebrews 9:12). One more act, final and unrepeatable, that completes what all the prior acts anticipated.
2: Israel is to ask the Egyptians for articles of silver and gold. The 'plundering of Egypt' begins here — the enslaved people leave with the wealth of their enslavers, fulfilling the Abrahamic promise (Genesis 15:14). Paul in Colossians 2:15 describes the cross in similar terms: Christ 'disarmed the powers and authorities, making a public spectacle of them, triumphing over them by the cross' — the ultimate plundering of the enemy's domain, where death itself is stripped of its power.
3: YHWH made the Egyptians favorably disposed toward the Israelites, and Moses was highly regarded in Egypt. The liberator gains honor in the enemy's camp. Jesus similarly drew the crowds: 'the large crowd listened to him with delight' (Mark 12:37). But the honor does not prevent the final confrontation — Moses's honored status in Egypt does not soften Pharaoh's heart; Jesus's popularity does not prevent the crucifixion. The favor of the crowd and the opposition of the authorities coexist until the decisive moment.
4: 'About midnight I will go throughout Egypt.' The divine announcement of the night of judgment — the most significant night in Israel's history. Jesus was arrested, tried, and condemned in the night before his crucifixion; the events of Holy Thursday-Friday unfolded in the night hours. Paul in Romans 13:12 uses the dawn/night imagery: 'The night is nearly over; the day is almost here' — the Christian life is lived in the eschatological night between the first and second comings, awaiting the dawn of resurrection.
5: 'Every firstborn son in Egypt will die, from Pharaoh's firstborn to the slave girl's firstborn, and all the firstborn of the livestock.' The death of the firstborn is the most comprehensive of all the plagues — no household in Egypt exempt. The firstborn represents the best, the heir, the continuation of the family. Christ, the 'firstborn over all creation' (Colossians 1:15) and the 'firstborn from the dead' (Colossians 1:18), bears this universal judgment: he is the firstborn who dies so that the firstborn of God's people might live. The exchange pattern — Egypt's firstborn for Israel's firstborn — is the type of the cross: the death of the Son so that the 'many sons and daughters' might come to glory (Hebrews 2:10).
6: 'There will be loud wailing throughout Egypt — worse than there has ever been or ever will be.' The unparalleled mourning at the death of the firstborn anticipates the mourning at the cross: the women of Jerusalem wept for Jesus (Luke 23:27-28); the disciples scattered in grief; the whole creation groaned. But Jesus warns the weeping women: 'Do not weep for me, weep for yourselves and for your children' — the cross creates grief, but that grief will be turned to joy (John 16:20-22).
7: 'But among the Israelites, not even a dog will bark at any person or animal.' Complete exemption of the covenant people from the plague. Romans 8:1: 'There is now no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus.' Those marked by the Passover blood are exempt not merely from one night's plague but from the final judgment: 'I tell you, whoever hears my word and believes him who sent me has eternal life and will not be judged but has crossed over from death to life' (John 5:24). No barking dog of condemnation reaches those under the blood.
8: 'All these officials of yours will come and bow down to me, saying, Go, you and all the people who follow you!' The officials who now resist will ultimately bow. Paul in Philippians 2:10-11: 'at the name of Jesus every knee should bow, in heaven and on earth and under the earth, and every tongue acknowledge that Jesus Christ is Lord.' The forced acknowledgment that Pharaoh's court will make at the final plague is the type of the ultimate universal acknowledgment at the name of the risen Christ.
9: YHWH had said to Moses that Pharaoh would not listen, 'so that my wonders may be multiplied in Egypt.' The divine purpose is transparent: the resistance serves the revelation. In John 9, the disciples ask why a man was born blind; Jesus answers, 'This happened so that the works of God might be displayed in him.' The hardness of Pharaoh and the blindness of the man from birth are both incorporated into the divine purpose of display: God's power and mercy are made visible against the backdrop of human limitation and resistance.
10: 'Moses and Aaron performed all these wonders before Pharaoh, but the LORD hardened Pharaoh's heart.' The wonders are completed — the testimony is full. John 12:37: 'Even after Jesus had performed so many signs in their presence, they still would not believe in him.' The hardening of those who witness the signs without believing is the most sobering pattern in both Testaments. Signs authenticate; they do not coerce. Faith is not produced mechanically by miracles but by the Spirit's work through the proclaimed word (Romans 10:17).
